coredata.dc:title: Dye-doped electrospun fiber-based random lasers: The influence of combined gain media
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Carvalho, Alyson J.A.
Gonçalves, Igor M.
Santos, Emanuel P.
Pincheira, Pablo I.R.
Araujo, Paloma M.S.
de Oliveira, Helinando P.
Gomes, Anderson S.L.
coredata.dc:description: This article reports the development and characterization of polymeric Random Lasers (RLs) based on dye-doped electrospun fibers using Eudragit L-100 polymer doped with different gain media: Rodamine B (RhB), carbon dots (CD) and coumarin (CR). The fibers were prepared via electrospinning, resulting in ribbon-like or flat fibers with an average width of 500 nm, acting as the scattering medium and as the gain medium necessary to stimulate light amplification. The RL emission was initially investigated in purely rhodamine-doped fibers (EDGT-RhB), a well-documented system in the literature. Subsequently, we explored the RL emission in fibers doped with CD (EDGT-RhB-CD) and CR (EDGT-RhB-CR), proposed as combined gain media to enhance the performance of the resulting flexible RLs. The samples were excited by the second harmonic of a Q-switched nanosecond pulsed laser operating at 10 Hz. The fluorescence and RL emissions were monitored around 600 nm. The scattered emission was captured using two distinct detection channels: one collected the emission from the front surface of the sample (R). In contrast, the other collected the emission from the opposite surface (T). All samples demonstrated threshold RL behavior, with the EDGT-RhB-CR sample exhibiting the lowest threshold at approximately 5.14 μJ/pulse and a bandwidth narrowing to less than 10 nm in both R and T detection channels. A comprehensive analysis of the Fast Fourier Transforms of the RL spectra was conducted to obtain insights into the optical path lengths contributing to the lasing emissions. Additionally, the spatial intensity distribution profiles of the RL action were monitored using a CCD camera. Degradation measurements were also conducted to assess the combined gain media influence on the fibers’ RL emissions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dye-doped flexible random lasers
Eudragit electrospun fibers
Organic dyes
Carbon dots
Organic flexible random laser

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of electrospun fibers of RhB, RhB + CD, and RhB + CR
RL action in pure EDGT-RhB fibers

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Brazilian funding agencies CAPES, FINEP, FACEPE and CNPq. Pablo I. R. Pincheira acknowledges support from FONDECYT N\u25E6 11231075.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorbance spectra. (a) UV–vis absorption of CDs in aqueous solution. (b) Electrospun fibers (pristine EDGT – in black, EDGT-RhB - pink, EDGT-RhB-CD – green, EDGT-RhB-CR - red, and EDGT-CD-blue).

(a, b, c) SEM images of electrospun EDGT-RhB fibers. (d) The average tape width distribution. The average width computed is 2 μm. (e) The average tape thickness distribution. The average tape thickness is 500 nm.

(a) Schematic view of the experimental setup RL action characterization (out of scale). A Q-switched Nd:YAG pulsed laser (Continuum Surelite SL II-10, not shown) is used as an excitation source. (b) Optical microscopic image of a typical sample prepared for RL experiments. (c) Highlighting the degradation of the sample after pump (∼5 min). (d) SEM image showing the morphology of the nanoribbons itemized in Fig. 2.

Pure EDGT-RhB fibers RL results from R channel. (a) Normalized emission spectra before the RL threshold (4.81 μJ), at the threshold (6.24 μJ), and after the threshold (67.08 μJ). (b) FWHM dependence on EPE (hollow squares) and input-output experimental data (hollow circles). (c) 3D RL transversal spatial intensity profile above threshold at 67.08 μJ (inserts: Left, 2D intensity distribution below threshold; right, 2D intensity distribution profile around the threshold. (d) Dye photodegradation measurements: FWHM and RL emission peak intensity versus number of shots. The solid lines are smooths of the experimental data to emphasize the curves' tendencies.

EDGT-RhB-CD fibers RL results from R channel. (a) Normalized emission spectra before RL threshold (4.81 μJ), near (6.24 μJ), and after threshold (67.08 μJ). (b) FWHM dependence on EPE (hollow squares) and input-output experimental data (hollow circles). (c) 3D RL spatial intensity distribution profile above threshold at 67.08 μJ (inserts: left, 2D intensity distribution below threshold; right, 2D intensity distribution profile near RL threshold. (d) Photodegradation measurements: FWHM and RL emission peak intensity versus number of shots. The solid lines are smooths of the experimental data to emphasize the curves' tendencies.

EDGT-RhB-CR fibers RL results from R channel. (a) Normalized emission spectra before RL threshold (4.81 μJ), after RL threshold for low EPE level (6.24 μJ), and high (67.08 μJ). (b) FWHM dependence on EPE (hollow squares) and input-output experimental data (hollow circles). (c) 3D RL spatial intensity distribution profile above threshold at 67.08 μJ (inserts: Left, 2D intensity distribution below threshold; right, 2D intensity distribution profile after RL threshold low EPE regime. (d) Dye photodegradation measurements: FWHM and RL emission peak intensity versus number of shots. The solid lines are smooths of the experimental data to emphasize the curves' tendencies.

FFT analysis of RL emission spectra at 67.08 μJ for (a) EDGT-RhB, (b) EDGT- RhB-CD, and (c) EDGT-RhB-CR samples. Estimated OPLs are approximately 50 nm, 35 nm, and 70 nm, respectively, illustrating the effect of scattering on lasing feedback.

Comparison of Full Width Half Max (FWHM) and Optical Path Length (OPL) for energies 4.72 μJ (below threshold), 9.43 μJ (near threshold), and 67.80 μJ (above threshold). Increasing energy reduces both FWHM and OPL due to enhanced photon-scatterer interactions.

head.ce:title: Dye-doped electrospun fiber-based random lasers: The influence of combined gain media

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Instituto de Pesquisa em Ciencia dos Materiais, Universidade Federal do Vale do São Francisco, Av. Antônio C. Magalhães, 510, Santo Antonio, Juazeiro, 48902-300, Bahia, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article reports the development and characterization of polymeric Random Lasers (RLs) based on dye-doped electrospun fibers using Eudragit L-100 polymer doped with different gain media: Rodamine B (RhB), carbon dots (CD) and coumarin (CR). The fibers were prepared via electrospinning, resulting in ribbon-like or flat fibers with an average width of 500 nm, acting as the scattering medium and as the gain medium necessary to stimulate light amplification. The RL emission was initially investigated in purely rhodamine-doped fibers (EDGT-RhB), a well-documented system in the literature. Subsequently, we explored the RL emission in fibers doped with CD (EDGT-RhB-CD) and CR (EDGT-RhB-CR), proposed as combined gain media to enhance the performance of the resulting flexible RLs. The samples were excited by the second harmonic of a Q-switched nanosecond pulsed laser operating at 10 Hz. The fluorescence and RL emissions were monitored around 600 nm. The scattered emission was captured using two distinct detection channels: one collected the emission from the front surface of the sample (R). In contrast, the other collected the emission from the opposite surface (T). All samples demonstrated threshold RL behavior, with the EDGT-RhB-CR sample exhibiting the lowest threshold at approximately 5.14 μJ/pulse and a bandwidth narrowing to less than 10 nm in both R and T detection channels. A comprehensive analysis of the Fast Fourier Transforms of the RL spectra was conducted to obtain insights into the optical path lengths contributing to the lasing emissions. Additionally, the spatial intensity distribution profiles of the RL action were monitored using a CCD camera. Degradation measurements were also conducted to assess the combined gain media influence on the fibers’ RL emissions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Random Lasers (RLs) are nonlinear optical systems that amplify light via stimulated emission within scattering media, which provides optical feedback, distinguishing them from conventional laser setups typically reliant on Fabry-Perot mirror-based cavities. Seminal experiments documented in the literature have elucidated crucial characteristics of RL systems, including bandwidth narrowing and nonlinear enhancement of emission [1,2]. Many studies have been reported in the literature about RL across various photonic scattering materials, encompassing empirical investigations, theoretical analyses, and computational simulations [3]. In the context of experimental demonstration, computational simulations, and statistical theoretical studies of complex systems, RLs have already been used to investigate replica symmetry-breaking [4], Lévy statistics [5], turbulence [6], random-matrix- theory [7], machine learning [8], photonic Hall effect, and photonic magnetoresistance [9], as well as for a variety of applications, including cancer tissue diagnosis [10,11], tumor diagnosis [12], pH detection [13], flexible displays [14], among others [3,15,16].

In the search for prototypes that meet specific applications and generate RL efficiently, various materials have been reported in previous works that serve as gain media and scatterers. These materials span a wide range, encompassing rare earth ions doped matrices [17–19], naturally occurring substances [20,21], dyes [22–24], ceramics [25,26], human tissue [10], quantum dots [27,28], and other materials as documented in prior references [15,29]. The development of flexible materials is a big challenge that needs to be incorporated into developing more versatile optoelectronics applications. According to Ref. [30], flexible materials can be classified as polymers, biomaterials, organic and inorganic materials, and hybrid structures. The relevance of random fiber lasers (RFLs), inherently flexible structures, is worth mentioning Ref. [31], where the authors presented the first RFL in a suspension of 250 nm rutile (TiO2) particles in a rhodamine 6G solution injected into the hollow core of a photonic crystal fiber. They demonstrated that, compared to similar systems in bulk format, the RFL exhibits an efficiency of at least two orders of magnitude higher. Moreover, in Ref. [32], the group utilized nanoparticles embedded in a high-quality ZnO film to create a flexible semiconductor laser in the UV region, while in Ref. [33], the authors studied the effects of in-plane coherent feedback in vertically aligned ZnO nanorods on a plastic substrate. In general, flexible random lasers (FRLs) can leverage diverse applications due to their deformability, flexibility, and the ability to be made in various sizes and shapes. Among the RFLs reported in the literature, the development of dye-doped electrospun fibers has been favored by simply incorporating additives (soluble organic dyes or dispersed inorganic nanoparticles) in the polymer solution. As an advantage, the massive production of flexible fiber nets with the homogeneous distribution of scattering centers and gain media into the bulky surface of the polymeric net is observed. These centers are distributed in 3D directions, forming random loops that improve the emissive radiation of light penetrating the fibers and transferring them along with their borders.

Herein, the RL action is investigated in membranes of Eudragit L-100 polymer fibers containing rhodamine B as the gain medium, combined with CD (EDGT-RhB-CD) and CR (EDGT-RhB-CR) as additives, aiming to a potential flexible material to generate more efficient RL emission around 630 nm. The RL action was evaluated from both the detection channels R and T. Spectral narrowing was observed in each sample with increased pump energy, indicating the RL behavior. Moreover, the Fast Fourier transform (FFT) of the RL spectra provided insights into the effective optical path lengths contributing to each system's RL action. Furthermore, evaluating the materials' RL performance requires assessing the spatial intensity distribution of the RL emissions. Additionally, we measured the photodegradation time responses of the samples to evaluate their stability and durability under prolonged exposure. These combined analyses provided an in-depth understanding of the materials' RL emission properties.

Eudragit L-100 (EDGT) (Evonik, Germany), rhodamine B (RhB) (Dinâmica, Brazil), coumarin-460 (CR, Exciton), ethanol, and acetic acid were used as received. Ciprofloxacin (Aldrich, USA) was processed as a powder to facilitate weighing and dissolution in the solution.

CD were hydrothermally converted from ciprofloxacin, which consists of an antibiotic from the quinolone family. CD nanoparticles were prepared following the procedure: 0.01 g of ciprofloxacin was dissolved in 5 mL of acetic acid, and 25 mL of deionized water was incorporated into the mixture. The solution was sonicated in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min. The solution was thermally treated at 180 ◦ C for 8 h in a stainless-steel autoclave lined with PTFE, with the resulting solution cooled to room temperature and dialyzed through a dialysis membrane with deionized water for 2 h to remove the antibiotic that did not react during the hydrothermal process [34]. The resulting solution was stored in a refrigerator for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of electrospun fibers of RhB, RhB + CD, and RhB + CR
ce:section.ce:para: Three solutions were prepared at fixed concentrations of EDGT, which were associated with the pure RhB and combined presence of RhB and CDs. For all groups of samples, the solution was prepared as follows: 1.2 g of EDGT was dispersed in 6 mL of ethanol and kept under stirring for 5 min. For the association with RhB, the dye was added at a concentration of 10 mM and kept under stirring for another 5 min. The preparation of the solution with CDs was conducted with the same steps in addition to a following step in which 500 μL of the resulting solution of CD was added to the alcoholic solution of EDGT and again kept under stirring for an additional 5 min [35]. The RhB + CR samples were prepared by incorporating 10 mM of RhB into 6 mL of EDGT alcoholic solution with the dispersion of dye molecules at a stirring time of 5 min. CR was added at a concentration of 10 mM with an additional stirring time of 5 min.

As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the CD solution is characterized by peaks in UV (prominent peaks at 204 and 279 nm with a broadband at 330 nm). In terms of the response of electrospun fibers prepared with different combinations of additives (pristine, CD, and RhB + CD) – see Fig. 1b, it is possible to observe that EDGT fiber presents a peak around 210 nm that superposes with the central peak of CD. The characteristic response for RhB-based fibers is observed at 563 nm. In comparison, an increment at 283 nm is observed for CD-doped electrospun fiber, confirming the adequate incorporation of the components in mixed fibers. The response for sample EDGT-RhB-CR comprises the superposition of the characteristic peaks of RhB and a broadband spectrum around 350 nm characteristic of CR-460 [36]. The CD efficiently adhered to the EDGT fibers, as seen from the absorbance spectra in Fig. 1a and b, mainly due to the presence of the peak near 283 nm in the EDGT- RhB-CD absorbance spectrum (green curve in Fig. 1b) as evidence of the CD presence. Moreover, comparing the absorbance spectra of the EDGT-RhB-CD and EDGT-RhB samples (green and pink curves in Fig. 1b, respectively), the broadband absorbance signal around the peak at 563 nm is increased due to the CD particles adsorbed on the surfaces of the fibers. Although the absorption of pure CD in aqueous suspension is negligible in the visible spectral region (see Fig. 1a), the presence of the CD increases the losses by reflection and scattering due to the high refraction index contrast between the CD on the fibers surfaces and the air. For instance, for CDs suspended in water with diameters ranging from 300 to 500 nm, the refraction index in the visible ranges from 1.3 to 1.9 [37]. A broad absorbance band near 400 nm in the sample EDGT-RhB-CR (as shown in Fig. 1b) confirms the efficient incorporation of CR into the EDGT fiber matrix. As observed the organic additive CR maintains the same absorbance level as the EDGT-RhB-CD fibers.

The electrospun fibers were evaluated in terms of morphology and structure using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), illustrated in Fig. 2 a, (b), and (c) (sample: EDGT-RhB). SEM images validate that the resultant fibers exhibit a nanoribbon structure, whether prepared with or without additives (CDs or CR). The average width and thickness measure were approximately 2 μm and 500 nm as can be seen in Fig. 2d and (e), respectively. It favors light propagation within the fiber, creating natural feedback paths that enhance stimulated emission amplification. The experimental conditions for the preparation of electrospun fibers were conducted as follows: a syringe containing 3 mL of polymer solution associated with RhB, RhB-CR, and RhB-CD was kept under a fixed pressure providing a continuous flux of 1 mL per hour, with the dip of the spinneret disposed at 20 cm away from the grounded target with is composed by a plan plate coated with an aluminum sheet (20 cm × 20 cm). The external high voltage is connected with a positive terminal on a spinneret and a grounded target with a corresponding value of 10 kV high, allowing the jet of solution that leaves the tip of the needle to be deposited on the aluminum foil surface.

The RL characterization was performed with excitation from the second harmonic of a Q-switched Nd:YAG laser (Surelite, Continuum, SLII-10), operating at 10 Hz of repetition rate and ∼25 ns of temporal pulse width. A half-wave plate (HWP) and a polarizer beam splitter (PBS) were used to control the excitation laser's intensity. A beam splitter (BS) is placed such that it directs 10 % of the incident light to a photodetector preceded by a neutral density (ND) filter, to avoid saturation of the photodetector, while a Bi-convex 100 mm lens focuses the remaining incident light onto a spot of ∼200 μm of diameter to excite the samples. The experimental setup is depicted in Fig. 3 a and – as mentioned previously – includes two detection channels: one collecting the backscattered emissions from the front surface of the sample, labeled as R; and the other, collecting the scattered emissions from the opposite sample's surfaces, labeled as T. As the excitation beam is also reflected and transmitted through the samples, color filters are placed in the spectrometers optical paths to cut off the excitation beam signal.

In the experimental arrangement (Fig. 3a), around 90 % of the light from the sample passes through a beam splitter and is collimated and focused onto the entrance of an optical fiber (Thorlabs, QP600-1-VIS-NIR) connected to a portable spectrometer (Ocean Optics Red Tide USB650 Spectrometer) with a spectral resolution of 1.0 nm. The remaining 10 % (from the beam splitter) is sent to a spectral camera (Thorlabs, BC106N-UV) with 1360 × 1024 pixels covering a sensitive area of 8.8 mm × 6.6 mm. This configuration allows simultaneous characterization of the fluorescence and RL emission and the spatial intensity distribution profiles for each excitation pulse energy (EPE) in the R and T channels.

Various works in the literature have reported RL action using dyes such as rhodamine as a gain medium [35,38]. It is a characteristic of dye-based lasers to exhibit high quantum efficiency in presenting stimulated emission at low pump energies and smooth emission curves, unlike other systems with a well-defined and much sharper threshold [39]. Thus, it is expected to achieve low energy thresholds for dye-based RL systems. We define as metrics to estimate the samples’ RL thresholds, the observation of the spectral width narrowing (the full width at half maximum – FWHM) as increases the EPE, and the evaluation of the input-output experimental curves. Additionally, the mode prevalence can be verified by directly analyzing the optical spectra. It is worth mentioning that there are other ways to define the RL threshold, as reviewed in Ref. [3]. Moreover, for each EPE applied to the samples, 100 spectra were recorded with an integration time of 100 ms. For the RL emission characterization, the average spectrum of these 100 spectra was considered for each EPE. Furthermore, all the samples were prepared with a thickness L = (1.0 ± 0.5 mm), while the typical lengths required to the photons inside the material achieve the diffusive regime [40] should be on the order of the distances between nearby fibers, i.e., on the tens of micrometers (see Fig. 2). This ensures that our systems meet the multiple scattering requirements. This condition indicates that light undergoes various scattering events before being transmitted and scattered through the material, essential for achieving the RL action.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RL action in pure EDGT-RhB fibers
ce:section.ce:para: The pure EDGT-RhB fibers exhibit the typical RL action for systems based on RhB – see Fig. S1 in the supplementary material (SM). The RhB serves as a gain medium, then when the sample is excited with radiation at 532 nm (inside the absorbance band of the dye – see Fig. 1), a broadband fluorescent emission is expected, as one can observe in Fig. 4 a. Moreover, when the EPE is increased, more fluorescence photons are generated. Due to the EDGT fiber's random arrangement (see Fig. 2), a combination of multiple scattering and probably waveguide mechanisms [41] enhances the probability of some photons remaining within the material's gain volume for extended periods, then increasing the stimulated emission of a photonic mode. For pure EDGT-RhB fibers, the EPE was varied in the range of 4.72 μJ–116.92 μJ (as can be checked in Fig. S1), whereas at 6.24 μJ, a peak starts to appear around 630 nm (green curve in Fig. 4a). Based on the FWHM versus EPE and the input-output curve, the RL emission threshold of the material is established to be 6.24 μJ, as confirmed by the data presented in Fig. 4b. Above the EPE threshold, the spectral bandwidth around 630 nm narrows to an average FWHM of ∼17 nm (see Fig. 4b).

To have a whole picture of the EDGT-RhB RL action, we measured the spatial intensity distribution of the RL emission to evaluate how the RL scattered light is distributed around the sources (EDGT fibers). From the 3D intensity distribution shown in Fig. 4c, it is possible to notice that at an EPE of 67.08 μJ (above the RL threshold), there are mainly two higher intensity points near the center of the distribution, explaining the efficiency increasing in the input-output curve (Fig. 4b). We performed measurements at intensity levels below the RL threshold (4.81 μJ), at the threshold (6.24 μJ), and above it (67.08 μJ). Fig. 4c displays two-dimensional (2D) and three-dimensional (3D) images of the RL intensity distribution of EDGT- RhB fibers under different EPEs. To measure the dye photodegradation time, the EDGT-RhB sample was excited at ∼100 μJ (above the threshold) for approximately 5 min (corresponding to 3.0 × 103 laser shots). The RL intensity decreases (red line in Fig. 4d) while the FWHM starts to increase, reaching an apparent stability width of ∼17 nm (black line in Fig. 4d).

It is important to emphasize that the light intensity emitted by an RL system is expected to be non-isotropic due to the random paths of photons within the material, leading to angular intensity dependency [42,43] of the RL emission. Monitoring the RL action from the T channel confirms variations in RL peak intensity compared to the R channel results (compare Fig. S1 and Fig. S4 of the SM). However, the RL spectral shape remains unchanged when measured from the T channel. Additionally, the RL peak mode and FWHM narrowing are consistent across both channels (R and T). This behavior was consistently observed across all samples evaluated in this study, making it significant for future applications as it supports the development of systems for intensity control.

Fig. 5 presents the results characterizing the light scattered by the EDGT-RhB-CD fibers through channel R. Fig. 5a shows the RhB broad-band fluorescent spectrum at low EPE, near the RL threshold at 6.24 μJ (mode competition is still present) and above (67.08 μJ). Even as for the pure EDGT-RhB sample, for the EDGT-RhB-CD sample, the RL mode also occurs around 630 nm, confirming that the gain is due to the RhB. The RL action threshold for the EDGT-RhB-CD fibers occurs when the EPE reaches the value of 7.16 μJ. From this EPE value, the mode competition ends, and the mode around 625 nm starts to be amplified (see Fig. S2 of the SM). The results from the experimental curves in Fig. 5b corroborate this threshold value.

As discussed, the presence of the CDs enhances the fluorescence scattering, resulting in strong interaction between photons inside the gain volume, then increasing the RL peak intensity. As a result, it is expected to have a RL emission with a narrow bandwidth compared to the EDGT-RhB sample. From Fig. 5b, one can confirm this statement, verifying that the FWHM narrow of the EDGT-RhB-CD fibers goes to ∼12 nm, i.e., 30 % less than that achieved by the pure EDGT-RhB fibers. Consequently, the 3D spatial intensity distribution of the RL action (see Fig. 5c) is more Gaussian-like, presenting a very low background compared to the RL action distribution in pure EDGT-RhB fibers. Once again, as expected for an RL system, due to the anisotropy of the RL intensity distribution a difference between the R and T channels is expected (compare Figs. S2 and S5 of the SM). Thereby, the CD on the EDGT-RhB-CD fibers increases scattering, causing an accentuated mode competition (see spectrum for EPE equals to 7.16 μJ – Fig. S2 of the SM), making a higher EPE necessary to generate RL action. Moreover, due to the presence of CDs on the fiber surfaces, a waveguide mechanism [41] can also take place among nearby fibers, requiring a higher EPE to generate RL action. Overall, the addition of CDs is expected to increase the RL emission threshold for the EDGT-RhB-CD fibers.

Regarding the dye photodegradation analysis (see Fig. 5d), the RL peak intensity of the EDGT-RhB-CD fibers decreases by approximately 50 %. Additionally, the FWHM value increases until it reaches around 17 nm. Furthermore, no stability in the FWHM value was observed throughout the experiment. For the same time scale as the pure EDGT-RhB sample, the sample containing CDs maintains RL action efficiently with minimal spectral broadening, reaching the same FWHM value as the EDGT-RhB fibers, approximately 17 nm. However, the peak intensity levels are higher than those of the pure RhB sample. Therefore, the role of the CDs in the EDGT- RhB fibers is to increase the RL emission intensities while ensuring minimal spectral broadening.

Fig. 6 a shows the broadband fluorescence spectra of RhB under 532 nm excitation at low EPE, and the RL emission spectra around 630 nm at higher EPE. Data in Fig. 6b indicate that the RL action threshold for EDGT-RhB-CR fibers is near 5.38 μJ, with the 630 nm RL mode prevailing. Post-threshold, the spectral narrowing is less than 10 nm. The 3D spatial intensity distribution profile in Fig. 6c reveals a RhB fluorescence background and a nearly Gaussian distribution with sharp peaks, characteristic of RL systems with mode competition still occurring. These results are similar to those of the EDGT-RhB sample, achieving comparable peak intensities. Dye photodegradation measurements (Fig. 6d) show that EDGT-RhB-CR maintains near-stability in spectral intensity and bandwidth, with only a 20 % decrease in RL emission intensity, compared to pure EDGT-RhB and EDGT-RhB-CD fibers. This result is promising, as it suggests that an organic additive like CR could be an excellent candidate for a dye dual-mode lasing material, ensuring efficient RL action when excited at 532 nm, as will be discussed.

The performance of EDGT-RhB-CR fibers in RL action can be attributed to the incorporation of CR as an additive. The overlap in the emission of CR and the absorption of RhB facilitates efficient energy transfer between the two dyes. When EDGT-RhB-CR fibers are excited at 532 nm under low EPE conditions, it results solely in RhB fluorescence. As the EPE increases, more RhB molecules enter the excited state. The proximity of the CR and RhB dye molecules – achieved by using the same molar concentrations (10 mM) and the uniform distribution ensured by the electrospinning method – further enhances the probability of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [44]. FRET is a non-radiative energy transfer mechanism that can occur between two chromophores: a donor and an acceptor [45] (in our case, RhB and CR, respectively). Thus, some CR molecules excited by the FRET mechanism emit photons in the visible range that RhB molecules can absorb due to the overlap of the absorption and emission spectra. This means that, in the same EPE regime as the pure EDGT-RhB sample, the EDGT-RhB-CR fibers provide more photons in the visible range near 532 nm. Consequently, increasing the non-resonant feedback enhances the gain and reduces the energy required for the RL action, resulting in a lower RL threshold.

Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is a key tool for analyzing RL systems, as it resolves their intricate spectral features and identifies dominant optical resonances [46,47]. In RLs, narrow emission spikes overlaying amplified spontaneous emission (ASE) arise from optical resonators of varying sizes. FFT transforms these spectra into the frequency domain, enabling the determination of cavity dimensions and providing insights into the system's dynamics.

The relationship between Optical Path Length (OPL) and RL is examined using FFT analysis of laser emission spectra. The frequency domain representation highlights resonant peak separations, allowing the wave number differences (Δk) determination, analogous to the Free Spectral Range (FSR) in conventional lasers. This Δk-OPL relationship reflects photon propagation and dispersion within the medium. Furthermore, the inverse FFT amplitude indicates the system's frequency response, revealing less effective resonances and aiding in OPL estimation.

FFT analysis of RL spectra at 67.08 μJ (Fig. 7 ) reveals OPLs of ∼50 nm for EDGT-RhB, ∼35 nm for EDGT-RhB-CD, and ∼70 nm for EDGT-RhB- CR. The shorter OPL for EDGT-RhB-CD indicates dominant incoherent feedback due to increased scattering, while the longer OPL for EDGT-RhB- CR reflects reduced scattering, resulting in lower thresholds and improved photon propagation. These findings highlight the impact of scattering properties on lasing feedback mechanisms. Detailed FFT procedures are described in the SM.

Table 1 compares the FWHM and OPL for pump energies of 4.72 μJ (below threshold), 9.43 μJ (near threshold), and 67.8 μJ (above threshold). As energy increases, FWHM and OPL decrease due to higher photon density, leading to enhanced photon-scatterer interactions. This narrows the emission spectrum, reduces FWHM, and limits photon travel due to increased scattering and absorption.

In this work, we studied the influence of combining multiple gain media, CD and CR, on the RL action of EDGT-RhB fibers. It was verified that the presence of these additives in the EDGT-RhB fibers alters the material's scattering levels and gain properties, thereby affecting the optical paths of fluorescence photons. This results in different mode competition dynamics directly influencing the RL emission. For the pure EDGT-RhB fibers, the RL emission occurs near 625 nm with the threshold for an EPE of 6.24 μJ. The spectral bandwidth is reduced to an average FWHM of 17 nm, and this value remains stable for approximately 5 min without spectral broadening. In the case of the EDGT-RhB-CR sample, RL emission also occurs around 630 nm, similar to the EDGT-RhB and EDGT-RhB-CD fibers. However, adding CR modifies the scattering and gain properties, resulting in a distinct RL threshold and emission dynamics. The RL threshold for the EDGT-RhB-CR fibers is approximately 6.24 μJ. The spectral narrowing around 630 nm achieves an FWHM less than 10 nm, narrower than the EDGT-RhB fibers and comparable to the EDGT-RhB-CD fibers. Although there is a decrease in emission intensity by about 20 % over time, indicating some dyes photodegradation, nevertheless the EDGT-RhB-CR fibers exhibit relatively stable performance with minimal spectral broadening. This suggests that CR contributes effectively to RL action, albeit with slightly different dynamics than CDs. Overall, incorporating CR into the EDGT-RhB fibers enhances RL performance, providing a viable or improved alternative to both pure electrospun EDGT-RhB and EDGT-RhB-CD fibers. From a materials science perspective, the electrospun EDGT-RhB-CR fibers show promise as a flexible organic RL source due to their straightforward fabrication process, ensuring efficient RL emission around 630 nm and compatibility with the dye's dual-mode gain medium and adsorption of nanoparticles to increase scatter as the CD. Furthermore, the EDGT-RhB-CR fibers demonstrate excellent RL emission stability, with no spectral broadening observed over 5 min, and operate at high peak intensity levels, making them suitable for a wide range of photonic applications. FFT analysis further elucidates the relationship between OPL and lasing feedback mechanisms, highlighting how scattering properties affect photon propagation and spectral dynamics. By resolving the RL spectra into the frequency domain, FFT provides insights into resonant peak separations and the Δk-OPL relationship, underscoring the interplay between gain medium composition, scattering behavior, and optical feedback. Together, these findings advance the understanding of RL systems and establish EDGT-RhB-CR fibers as a promising material for photonic applications.

Alyson J.A. Carvalho: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Igor M. Gonçalves: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Emanuel P. Santos: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Pablo I.R. Pincheira: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Paloma M.S. Araujo: Methodology, Investigation. Helinando P. de Oliveira: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Anderson S.L. Gomes: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

11231075.
